coredata.dc:title: Application of green synthesized TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite as high efficient antibacterial agent against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.dc:creator: Teymourinia, Hakimeh
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Amiri, Omid
Yazdian, Fatemeh
coredata.dc:description: TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite was prepared by solvothermal method to study its antibacterial properties. XRD, SEM, EDX, FT-IR and TEM were used to characterize prepared nanomaterials. To evaluate effect of graphene quantum dots (GQD) on the antibacterial activity of final products, antibacterial activity of TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQDs were compared with TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs. TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs shows the highest antibacterial activity against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs show the lowest measure the minimal inhibitory concentration against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. MIC for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.03 and 0.1, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs
Nanostructures
Antibacterial
Nanostructures
Solvothermal
Quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Studying antibacterial effect of as-prepared nanosized materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD of Sb2S3/GQDs and (b) EDX of Sb2S3/GQDs.
(a) XRD of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs and (b) EDX of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs.
(a, b) SEM of Sb2S3/GQDs and (c, d) SEM of Sb2S3/GQDs.
TEM images of (a) GQDs and (b) TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs composite.
FT-IR spectra of (a) sb2S3, (b) Sb2S3/GQDs, (c) TiO2/GQDs and (d) TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs.
SEM images of (a, b) pure TiO2, (c, d) Sb2S3 and (e) TiO2/GQDs.
XRD of (a) pure Sb2S3 and (b) pure TiO2.
Mechanism for improving in antibacterial activity of (a) TiO2/GQDs and (b)TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs.
Optical Density (O.D) for growing E. coli and Staphylococcus in presence of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs.
The MIC values for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus bacterial against as synthesized nanocomposites.
The MIC values for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus bacterial against for pervious reports.

head.ce:title: Application of green synthesized TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite as high efficient antibacterial agent against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemistry Department, College of Science, University of Raparin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of New Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite was prepared by solvothermal method to study its antibacterial properties. XRD, SEM, EDX, FT-IR and TEM were used to characterize prepared nanomaterials. To evaluate effect of graphene quantum dots (GQD) on the antibacterial activity of final products, antibacterial activity of TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQDs were compared with TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs. TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs shows the highest antibacterial activity against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs show the lowest measure the minimal inhibitory concentration against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. MIC for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.03 and 0.1, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Infectious diseases caused by bacteria are among the most important health challenges worldwide, which cause serious problem for millions of people annually [1,2]. Using antibiotic is main way to kill bacteria, which can cause illness and disease. Many diseases that once killed people can now be treated effectively with antibiotics. However, some bacteria have become resistant to commonly used antibiotics and limit the effectiveness of antibiotic treatments for infectious diseases [3–5]. Antibiotic resistant bacteria are bacteria that are not controlled or killed by antibiotics and able to survive and even multiply in the presence of an antibiotic [6]. Unfortunately, most infection-causing bacteria can become resistant to some antibiotics. Today, antibiotic resistance becomes a public health problem. So, it is necessary to develop efficient antibacterial material and techniques [7]. Among them, nanoscale materials have emerged as novel antibacterial and antimicrobial agents [8]. Especially, several classes of antibacterial and antimicrobial nanoparticles and Nano-sized carriers for antibiotics delivery have proven their effectiveness for treating infectious diseases, including antibiotic-resistant ones [8,9]. Nanoparticles can improved properties to classical organic antibacterial agents because of their high surface area to volume ratio, resulting in appearance of new mechanical, chemical, electrical, optical, magnetic, electro-optical, and magneto-optical properties of the NPs that are different from their bulk properties [10]. In this case, nano sized materials have been demonstrated to be interesting in the context of Figurehting bacteria [11].

Recently, researchers report that silver nanoparticles or silver salts could effectively destroy antibiotic resistant bacteria [6,12,13]. For example, Panáček et al. [15] show that silver nanoparticles destroy multiresistant Enterobacteriaceae [14]. However, in late 2017, Aleš Panáček and et al. declare that Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli 013, Pseudomonas aeruginosa CCM 3955 and E. coli CCM 3954 can develop resistance to silver nanoparticles [16].

Here we introduced a novel and high efficient antibacterial nano-composite with the promising low minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs), 0.03 for E. Coli and 0.1 for Staphylococcus aureus. We increase antibacterial properties of TiO2 nanoparticles in two steps. First, Sb2S3 was added to robust antibacterial activity of TiO2. Sb2S3 is a semiconductor with bandgap of 1.5 eV and conduction band higher to those of TiO2. Sb2S3 has recently attracted great research attention due to its suitable direct band-gap and high absorption coefficient. So, crystalline Sb2S3 is an ideal catalyst for photo-degradation of any organic pollutant [16,17].

Previous reports claim that species such as hydroxyl groups, superoxide ions, singlet oxygen, protonated superoxide radical [18–22]. We believe that Sb2S3 can act as sensitizer and increase produce hydroxyl groups, superoxide ions, singlet oxygen, and protonated superoxide radical [23]. In the next step, we added graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and prepared TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs to increase as much as the antibacterial activity of TiO2. GQDs are carbon nanosheets of <20 nm [24,25] and have recently attracted great research attention due to their unique properties [26,27].

Graphene oxide and other graphene derivatives have been impregnated into paper and cotton fabrics for practical applications and exhibit antibacterial activity against e.g. common dental pathogens, illustrating their promise in the biomedical field [28,29]. The graphene and other graphene derivatives induced cell damage is related to both physical and chemical effects. While the chemical effect involves an oxidative stress induced by the generation of reactive oxygen species, oxidation of cellular components, and charge transfer, the physical effect is related to the cell damage caused by the sharp edges of the graphene nanosheets [30,31] Additionally, direct interactions of graphene-based materials with cell membranes have been also reported as leading to a loss of the bacterial membrane integrity [32,33]. In the case of GQDs, the bactericidal property highly depends on the diversity of surface functional groups and on surface charges. Therefore, a reduced cell viability was related to the degradation of the bacterial membranes caused by the strong electrostatic interactions between negatively charged O– containing functional groups and positively charged lipids of the cell membranes [34,35].

Here we present for the first time a report on the antibacterial behavior of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs in the colloidal suspension for achieve very low MIC. To better understand the health and environmental impacts of this new nano-composite, the antibacterial activity of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs toward two bacterial models - Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus was studied and compared TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, TiO2/Sb2S3 and Sb2S3/GQDs.

In order to characterize as-prepared nanomaterials, Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were used. Based on these results, we introduce TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs as new antibacterial nanomaterials.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesis according to our pervious works [36,37]. Briefly, 0.25 g of corn powder was dispersed into 25 mL DI water and stirred for 30 min. Then the solution was transferred into a Teflon lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 48 h. It should be notice that GQDs are soluble in water. GC-2550TG (Teif Gostar Faraz Company, Iran) were used for all chemical analyses.

0.25 g of SbCl3 was added into 30 mL of 0.045 M of GQDs solution and stirred for 10 min. Then 30 mL of glycol was introduced and stirred continuously for 10 min. Afterward this obtained solution together with 0.2 g of sulfur powder and 0.3 g of NaBH4 was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and placed in oven at 180 °C for 3 days. Resulting products washed with distilled water and ethanol three times.

In a typical synthesis, as-synthesized Sb2S3/GQDs were dispersed into 30 of 0.045 M of graphene quantum dots solution and sonicated for 15 min. 5 mL titanium tetraisopropoxide was mixed with ethanol in 1:5 ratio. The above solution was added dropwise under sonication and kept overnight with vigorous stirring. On the next day, whole solution was transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, sealed, and maintained at 140 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying antibacterial effect of as-prepared nanosized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of pure TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQDs, and TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite was evaluated by microdilution assays, as described by the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI). Here E. coli and S. aureus were used as model microorganisms in order to measure the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of as prepared nanostructures. The strains of bacteria were cultivated in Mueller-Hinton broth incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The bacterial suspensions were diluted with Mueller-Hinton broth to OD570 = 0.2 and were distributed (100 μL) in a 96-well plate. Each well was exposed to different concentrations of nanomaterials (from 0.01 to 0.89·mg·L−1) under visible irradiation. The plates were incubated at 37 °C, and the bacterial growth was observed after 18 h. The test was repeated three times and the MIC was considered as the lowest nanomaterial concentration where no bacterial growth was observed.

In present work, TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite was reported for the first time. We have found that this novel nanocomposite shows very high antibacterial effects. In order to prepare TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite, we first prepare water soluble GQDs from corn powder according to our pervious report (please see reference 37). Then we prepare Sb2S3/GQDs nanoribbons composite by using GQDs solution instead of DI water in reference 38. XRD pattern and EDX of Sb2S3/GQDs nanoribbons composite are presented in Fig. 1 . XRD pattern only shows Sb2S3. This could happen because of low amount of GQDs in this sample. So, EDX analysis was used. The EDX results approve presence of carbon element related to GQDs, antimony and sulfur element related to the Sb2S3 (Fig. 1b). XRD pattern also shows that Sb2S3 was crystalized in orthorhombic-phase (good agreement with JCPDS. Card file No. 06-0474). In the next step, Sb2S3/GQDs composite was used as precursor to prepare TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite.

We used GQD solution instead of water to increase ratio of GQDs in the final products. Fig. 2 represent XRD and EDX results for TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite. TiO2 can be indexed to JCPDS. Card file No. 71-1169. EDX result shows that Sb, C, Ti, S and O are exist in the prepared sample.

Electron scanning microscopy (SEM) was used to study the morphology of final products. SEM results are summarized in Fig. 3 . SEM images related to Sb2S3/GQDs are shown in the Fig. 3(a, b), it shows that as-synthesized Sb2S3/GQDs has ribbons like morphology with 70–100 nm in width and 2–5 μm in length. Also, Fig. 3c and d shows the SEM images of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite. It seems that TiO2 particles shaped around the Sb2S3/GQDs nanoribbons and formed large plate.

Fig. 4 shows TEM images of GQDs, TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs composite. As seen in the Fig. 4, size of prepared graphene quantum dots are about 10 nm. Also TEM of GQDs, TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs shows the presence of GQDs, TiO2 and Sb2S3. In Fig. 4b which is related to the nano-composite, rod like structures and spherical particles represent Sb2S3 and TiO2/GQDs, respectively.

FT-IR analysis was used to understand structures of the products and investigating functional groups of obtained Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQD, TiO2/GQD and TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs. Fig. 5a illustrated the FTIR spectra of the Sb2S3. As shown in Fig. 5a, there is not significant absorption bands because stretching vibration of SbS are located in far IR. Peak at 3417 cm−1 is imputed to the stretching vibration of water. By adding graphene quantum dots to the Sb2S3, one peak at 1283 cm−1 was appeared which can be ascribed to the nitro group (Fig. 5b).

It should be notice that other peaks related to GQDs do not appear because of low amount of it. Fig. 5c shows the FT-IR for TiO2/GQD. It should be noted, the peak around 400–900 cm−1 in TiO2 nanoparticles is related to TiOTi and TiOC bond in GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite. In addition to, in FT-IR spectra of TiO2/GQD nanocomposite peaks at 1863 and 3429 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC, OH and NH group, respectively. On the other side, in this spectra, it can be observed that absorption bands at 1091 is corresponded to the CN cm−1, which demonstrated the presence of phosphate groups of GQD on the surface of the GQDs in synthesized composites. Finally, Fig. 5d shows the FT-IR of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs which is almost same with those of TiO2/GQD because stretching vibration of SbS are located in far IR.

After the successfully synthesis of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite, its antibacterial properties was investigated. To better understanding the effect of GQDs on antibacterial activity prepared composite, antibacterial properties of pure TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQDs, and TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite were investigated. SEM images of pure TiO2, Sb2S3, TiO2/GQDs are shown in Fig. 6 .

As seen from the results, TiO2 particles have spherical shape with 50–70 nm in diameter (Fig. 6(a, b)). By adding GQDs during synthesis of TiO2, size of nanoparticles decreased to 10–20 nm while they still have spherical shape (Fig. 6(c, d)). Sb2S3 nanostructure has ribbon like structures with 200–300 in width and 3–4 μm in length (Fig. 6(e, f)). XRD of as-prepared nanostructures are shown in Fig. 7 . Base of their XRD pattern, TiO2 has amorphous structures. We synthesized TiO2 according to reference [39], where they report amorphous TiO2 [39]. Related composite still shows amorphous structures. In the case of Sb2S3, reflection peaks can be indexed to an orthorhombic-phase Sb2S3 (JCPDS Card File: 06–0474) and pure Sb2S3 is formed.

The antibacterial activity of pure TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3, Sb2S3/GQDs, and TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nanocomposite were explored against E. coli and S. aureus. The MIC values for these bacterial strains are tabled in Table 1 . As seen from table, we evaluate three concentrations for each nanomaterial. For pure TiO2, 0.08, 0.09, and 0.11 for E. coli and 0.53, 0.64, and 0.74 for Staphylococcus aureus were tested, respectively. There is no growth for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus observed in concentration of 0.11 and 0.74, respectively. By adding graphene quantum dots to the TiO2, MIC was decreased to the 0.09 and 0.4 for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. Etacheri et al. report that electron, hole and other spices such as OH, O2 and so on are responsible for killing bacteria [7]. Adding GQDs lead to produce more electron and hole pairs (Fig. 8a). Therefore, TiO2/GQDs shows higher antibacterial properties compare to the pure TiO2. MIC for bare GQDs is 0.1 and 0.5 for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively.

Adding GQDs to the Sb2S3 also improve its MIC by the same mechanism. Sb2S3 shows the MIC of 0.09 and 0.3 for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus, while Sb2S3/GQDs shows the MIC of 0.07 and 0.2 for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus, respectively. Finally, TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs shows the lowest MIC against E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus. MIC for E. coli and Staphylococcus aureus are 0.03 and 0.1, respectively. This highest antibacterial activity achieved because Sb2S3 and GQDs act as co-sensitizer and help TiO2 to produce more electron and holes (Fig. 8b). The primary step is generation of electron hole pairs. Electron in the conduction band can of GQDs, TiO2, and Sb2S3 can reduce O2 to produce superoxide radicals (O2 −). Further oxidation of O2 – by photogenerated holes from valance band of GQDs, TiO2, and Sb2S3 results in the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2). The hole in the valence band of GQDs, TiO2, and Sb2S3 can also react with H2O or OH– adsorbed on the surface of composite to produce hydroxyl radicals (OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and protonated superoxide radical (HO2). H2O2 in the valence band of GQDs, TiO2, and Sb2S3 is reported to be resulting from the coupling of two OH [40]. Further reaction of H2O2 with OH lead to form superoxide radical (HO2) that functions like O2 – to inactivate the bacterial cells [41]. Thus OH is suggested to play a crucial role in the production of H2O2 and HO2 in the valence band of GQDs, TiO2, and Sb2S3. The generated electron-hole pairs and the reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced from their reaction with H2O, OH−, and O2 results in the decomposition of bacteria. Optical density was measured and plotted as a function of time for 24 h for different prepared nanomaterials. Table 2 compare the MIC for pervious reports. Fig. 9 shows optical density for growing E. coli and Staphylococcus in presence of TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs. Optical density of bacterial growth for E. coli and Staphylococcus were decreased over the time which showing the reduction of bacterial growth rate.

In this work, we prepared TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs for the first time. This prepared nano-composite shows very high antibacterial activity. In order to study the effect of graphene quantum dots on the antibacterial properties of prepared composite, antibacterial activity of pure TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3 and Sb2S3/GQDs against E. coli and S. aureus were investigated. MIC of 0.03 and 0.1 was obtained for TiO2/Sb2S3/GQDs nano-composite against E. coli and S. aureus which is much higher compare to those for pure TiO2, TiO2/GQDs, Sb2S3 and Sb2S3/GQDs. Adding GQDs lead to produce more electron and hole pairs which could be correspond to higher antibacterial activity of spices including GQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/122).
